text:
ORACLE is a grounded oracle — a question-answering archive that speaks only from
a fixed canon of written knowledge about Penn's universe. You ask it something;
it finds the passages in its archive that bear on the question, and it answers
using only those passages. If the archive holds no answer, ORACLE says the canon
is silent rather than guessing. It is built by Penn as a demonstration of
retrieval-augmented generation done honestly.
embedText:
ORACLE Itself — What ORACLE is

ORACLE is a grounded oracle — a question-answering archive that speaks only from
a fixed canon of written knowledge about Penn's universe. You ask it something;
it finds the passages in its archive that bear on the question, and it answers
using only those passages. If the archive holds no answer, ORACLE says the canon
is silent rather than guessing. It is built by Penn as a demonstration of
retrieval-augmented generation done honestly.

text:
Every question travels the same path. First the question is turned into a vector
— a string of numbers capturing its meaning — and compared against the vector of
every passage in the canon. The closest passages are retrieved. Those passages,
and nothing else, are handed to a language model along with a strict instruction:
answer only from this context, cite each claim, and refuse anything the context
does not support. The answer you read is assembled from the archive, not from the
model's memory of the wider world.
embedText:
ORACLE Itself — How ORACLE answers

Every question travels the same path. First the question is turned into a vector
— a string of numbers capturing its meaning — and compared against the vector of
every passage in the canon. The closest passages are retrieved. Those passages,
and nothing else, are handed to a language model along with a strict instruction:
answer only from this context, cite each claim, and refuse anything the context
does not support. The answer you read is assembled from the archive, not from the
model's memory of the wider world.

text:
The trust comes from the grounding, not from faith in the model. The model never
answers from its own training knowledge; it is fenced inside the retrieved
passages. Citations point back to the exact fragments used, so any claim can be
checked against its source. And when a question falls outside the canon, the
retrieval step returns nothing strong enough to use, and ORACLE declines instead
of inventing — the silence is a feature.
embedText:
ORACLE Itself — Why it can be trusted

The trust comes from the grounding, not from faith in the model. The model never
answers from its own training knowledge; it is fenced inside the retrieved
passages. Citations point back to the exact fragments used, so any claim can be
checked against its source. And when a question falls outside the canon, the
retrieval step returns nothing strong enough to use, and ORACLE declines instead
of inventing — the silence is a feature.

text:
ORACLE runs entirely on Cloudflare. The interface is a SvelteKit application
served from Cloudflare Pages. The thinking happens in Cloudflare Workers AI: one
model turns text into vectors for search, another writes the grounded answer. The
passage vectors are computed once and kept in Cloudflare KV so that every
question after the first is answered instantly. There are no outside services and
no API keys — the whole oracle lives at the edge.
embedText:
ORACLE Itself — What ORACLE is made of

ORACLE runs entirely on Cloudflare. The interface is a SvelteKit application
served from Cloudflare Pages. The thinking happens in Cloudflare Workers AI: one
model turns text into vectors for search, another writes the grounded answer. The
passage vectors are computed once and kept in Cloudflare KV so that every
question after the first is answered instantly. There are no outside services and
no API keys — the whole oracle lives at the edge.

text:
ORACLE's archive is a collection of written documents. Some are about Penn's own
work. Others are about the subjects he cares about: Rust for Linux, the Linux
kernel, distributed systems, Zelda speedrunning, Neuromancer and the cyberpunk it
started. Each document is split into sections, and each section is a passage
ORACLE can retrieve. To teach ORACLE something new, you add a document to the
canon; it knows nothing beyond what is written there, and that is the point.
embedText:
ORACLE Itself — The canon

ORACLE's archive is a collection of written documents. Some are about Penn's own
work. Others are about the subjects he cares about: Rust for Linux, the Linux
kernel, distributed systems, Zelda speedrunning, Neuromancer and the cyberpunk it
started. Each document is split into sections, and each section is a passage
ORACLE can retrieve. To teach ORACLE something new, you add a document to the
canon; it knows nothing beyond what is written there, and that is the point.

text:
Penn is a systems-track graduate-school applicant — a kernel and Rust learner who
thinks and talks at the level of design tradeoffs rather than recipes. His work
spans low-level systems (Rust-for-Linux kernel drivers, embedded bring-up) and a
sprawl of distinctive web projects, most of them deployed to Cloudflare Pages.
embedText:
About Penn — Who he is

Penn is a systems-track graduate-school applicant — a kernel and Rust learner who
thinks and talks at the level of design tradeoffs rather than recipes. His work
spans low-level systems (Rust-for-Linux kernel drivers, embedded bring-up) and a
sprawl of distinctive web projects, most of them deployed to Cloudflare Pages.

text:
He edits in vim, not VS Code — references to editing are in vim's idiom, commands
and plugins included. He prefers fewer clarifying questions: infer from context
and ship, and only stop to ask when an ambiguity is genuinely load-bearing. On
projects where he has handed over judgment — Wanderlost is the clearest example —
he wants continuous additive polish and trusts the design calls to be made
without check-ins.
embedText:

He edits in vim, not VS Code — references to editing are in vim's idiom, commands
and plugins included. He prefers fewer clarifying questions: infer from context
and ship, and only stop to ask when an ambiguity is genuinely load-bearing. On
projects where he has handed over judgment — Wanderlost is the clearest example —
he wants continuous additive polish and trusts the design calls to be made
without check-ins.

text:
Penn writes with a varied cadence and concrete specifics, no em dashes, and lets
hedges and gentle self-deprecation land naturally. A writing sample lives with
his applications. His resume is both a local PDF and a live site at
pcporterfield-cv.pages.dev/resume.

text:
He has opinions about gear. The default recommendation leans toward non-Pi
single-board computers — Radxa, BeagleBoard, Pine64 — and quality over price
within a budget, on the principle that the hardware should outlast the project he
bought it for. For logic analyzers he is a lifelong Saleae loyalist: the Saleae
Logic is the default rec, and alternatives only come up if he asks for them.
embedText:

bought it for. For logic analyzers he is a lifelong Saleae loyalist: the Saleae

text:
Across everything — Goolz, Wanderlost, PEEK, MUTE, the kernel quest — there is a
consistent sensibility: retro and eldritch textures, small voiceless or cursed
protagonists, terminals and constraints treated as aesthetic, and a refusal to
let interfaces over-explain themselves. The systems work and the art are the same
taste pointed at different layers of the stack.
embedText:
About Penn — The shape of the portfolio

Across everything — Goolz, Wanderlost, PEEK, MUTE, the kernel quest — there is a
consistent sensibility: retro and eldritch textures, small voiceless or cursed
protagonists, terminals and constraints treated as aesthetic, and a refusal to
let interfaces over-explain themselves. The systems work and the art are the same
taste pointed at different layers of the stack.

text:
A distributed system is a set of computers that cooperate over a network and have
to behave, to the outside, like one reliable thing. The difficulty is that the
network can drop messages, delay them, or deliver them out of order, and any
machine can fail at any moment, and no node can ever be certain whether another
node has died or is just slow. Leslie Lamport put the strangeness best: "A
distributed system is one in which the failure of a computer you didn't even know
existed can render your own computer unusable."
embedText:
Distributed Systems — The hard part

A distributed system is a set of computers that cooperate over a network and have
to behave, to the outside, like one reliable thing. The difficulty is that the
network can drop messages, delay them, or deliver them out of order, and any
machine can fail at any moment, and no node can ever be certain whether another
node has died or is just slow. Leslie Lamport put the strangeness best: "A
distributed system is one in which the failure of a computer you didn't even know
existed can render your own computer unusable."

text:
In a single program you can trust a clock and an order of events. Across machines
you cannot, because there is no shared now. Lamport's 1978 work on logical clocks
showed how to reason about the order of events using causality rather than
wall-clock time: if one event could have influenced another, it comes first, and if
they could not have affected each other, their order does not matter. Vector clocks
extend this to track causality between many nodes at once. Most of the field is, in
one way or another, about inventing a usable notion of order where physics refuses
to give you one.
embedText:
Distributed Systems — Time is a lie

In a single program you can trust a clock and an order of events. Across machines
you cannot, because there is no shared now. Lamport's 1978 work on logical clocks
showed how to reason about the order of events using causality rather than
wall-clock time: if one event could have influenced another, it comes first, and if
they could not have affected each other, their order does not matter. Vector clocks
extend this to track causality between many nodes at once. Most of the field is, in
one way or another, about inventing a usable notion of order where physics refuses
to give you one.

text:
Consensus is the problem of getting a group of nodes to agree on a single value,
even when some of them fail. It sounds simple and is brutally hard. The FLP result
proved that in a fully asynchronous network, no algorithm can guarantee consensus
if even one node may fail, which is why real systems lean on timeouts and
probabilities rather than perfect guarantees. Lamport's Paxos solved practical
consensus but became infamous for being difficult to understand. Raft, designed by
Diego Ongaro and John Ousterhout, set out to do the same job in a way humans could
actually follow, and largely succeeded; it is now the consensus algorithm in many
production systems.
embedText:
Distributed Systems — Agreeing on anything

Consensus is the problem of getting a group of nodes to agree on a single value,
even when some of them fail. It sounds simple and is brutally hard. The FLP result
proved that in a fully asynchronous network, no algorithm can guarantee consensus
if even one node may fail, which is why real systems lean on timeouts and
probabilities rather than perfect guarantees. Lamport's Paxos solved practical
consensus but became infamous for being difficult to understand. Raft, designed by
Diego Ongaro and John Ousterhout, set out to do the same job in a way humans could
actually follow, and largely succeeded; it is now the consensus algorithm in many
production systems.

text:
A few ideas get repeated until they become folklore. The CAP theorem, from Eric
Brewer, says that when the network partitions, a system must choose between staying
consistent and staying available; it cannot have both during the split. The Two
Generals Problem shows that two parties cannot achieve guaranteed agreement over an
unreliable channel, no matter how many messages they send. The Byzantine Generals
Problem asks how to agree when some participants are not just failing but actively
lying. And the line every engineer knows, attributed to Phil Karlton: "There are
only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things."
embedText:
Distributed Systems — The laws everyone quotes

A few ideas get repeated until they become folklore. The CAP theorem, from Eric
Brewer, says that when the network partitions, a system must choose between staying
consistent and staying available; it cannot have both during the split. The Two
Generals Problem shows that two parties cannot achieve guaranteed agreement over an
unreliable channel, no matter how many messages they send. The Byzantine Generals
Problem asks how to agree when some participants are not just failing but actively
lying. And the line every engineer knows, attributed to Phil Karlton: "There are
only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things."

text:
ELS911 is school-safety infrastructure work — wayfinding, mapping, and emergency
floor plans for large school campuses. There are two distinct things that share
the name and should not be confused: the marketing site (els911.com) and a
separate portal product.
embedText:
ELS911 and School Safety Mapping — The work

ELS911 is school-safety infrastructure work — wayfinding, mapping, and emergency
floor plans for large school campuses. There are two distinct things that share
the name and should not be confused: the marketing site (els911.com) and a
separate portal product.

text:
The scanning rig is an FJD Trion P1 — a SLAM scanner with no GNSS — paired with
the Trion Model v2.05 desktop software. The pipeline turns raw scans of big
schools into 2D floor plans exported as DXF.

A few hard-won software notes:

- **Floor Extract is a filter, not a planning tool** — it isolates a slice, it
  doesn't author the plan.
- Hidden cloud returns are recovered through Switch Display rather than lost.
- Georeferencing goes through Coordinate Transformation, but for indoor school
  plans it is usually unneeded.
embedText:
ELS911 and School Safety Mapping — Floor-plan scanning

The scanning rig is an FJD Trion P1 — a SLAM scanner with no GNSS — paired with
the Trion Model v2.05 desktop software. The pipeline turns raw scans of big
schools into 2D floor plans exported as DXF.

A few hard-won software notes:

- **Floor Extract is a filter, not a planning tool** — it isolates a slice, it
  doesn't author the plan.
- Hidden cloud returns are recovered through Switch Display rather than lost.
- Georeferencing goes through Coordinate Transformation, but for indoor school
  plans it is usually unneeded.

text:
The deliverable map PDFs follow NFPA conventions. The legend is a vertical list
with one row per marker type, and hallway markers collapse into chips. Markers
render exactly where they were placed — no automatic separation, no leader lines
— and icon sizes are kept small so natural clustering reads correctly instead of
being spread artificially.
embedText:
ELS911 and School Safety Mapping — The map PDF rendering

The deliverable map PDFs follow NFPA conventions. The legend is a vertical list
with one row per marker type, and hallway markers collapse into chips. Markers
render exactly where they were placed — no automatic separation, no leader lines
— and icon sizes are kept small so natural clustering reads correctly instead of
being spread artificially.

els911-portal codebase.

text:
Fluxcore is a VPP arbitrage project — a virtual power plant concept built as an
interview deliverable. A virtual power plant aggregates many small, distributed
energy resources (home batteries, EVs, flexible loads) and operates them as if
they were one dispatchable plant; arbitrage is the act of buying and storing
energy when it is cheap and discharging when it is expensive.
embedText:
Fluxcore — What it is

Fluxcore is a VPP arbitrage project — a virtual power plant concept built as an
interview deliverable. A virtual power plant aggregates many small, distributed
energy resources (home batteries, EVs, flexible loads) and operates them as if
they were one dispatchable plant; arbitrage is the act of buying and storing
energy when it is cheap and discharging when it is expensive.

text:
Goolz is the umbrella identity that ties Penn's web work together — "one hegemony"
spanning a root society site, a Windows 95 desktop revival, and a small games
arcade. It lives at goolz.org, with source at github.com/melonmelonz/goolz and a
working tree at ~/dev/goolz. The whole thing deploys to Cloudflare Pages.

The name is deliberately ridiculous and a little eldritch. Goolz is less a brand
than a vibe: lavender-town dread, dead-mall nostalgia, and the feeling of finding
software that shouldn't still be running.
embedText:
The Goolz Hegemony — What Goolz is

Goolz is the umbrella identity that ties Penn's web work together — "one hegemony"
spanning a root society site, a Windows 95 desktop revival, and a small games
arcade. It lives at goolz.org, with source at github.com/melonmelonz/goolz and a
working tree at ~/dev/goolz. The whole thing deploys to Cloudflare Pages.

The name is deliberately ridiculous and a little eldritch. Goolz is less a brand
than a vibe: lavender-town dread, dead-mall nostalgia, and the feeling of finding
software that shouldn't still be running.

text:
The hegemony has three surfaces under one roof:

- **Goolz Society** — the root of goolz.org. The front door to everything.
- **Next Chapter** — a Windows 95 desktop revival served at /next-chapter. It
  absorbed the old standalone "Next Chapter Daily" project (NCD) in April 2026;
  NCD no longer ships on its own.
- **Playing With Goolz** — a games arcade at /games/pwg, also embedded inside the
  Win95 desktop as a fake executable called GOOLZ.exe.
embedText:

The hegemony has three surfaces under one roof:

- **Goolz Society** — the root of goolz.org. The front door to everything.
- **Next Chapter** — a Windows 95 desktop revival served at /next-chapter. It
  absorbed the old standalone "Next Chapter Daily" project (NCD) in April 2026;
  NCD no longer ships on its own.
- **Playing With Goolz** — a games arcade at /games/pwg, also embedded inside the
  Win95 desktop as a fake executable called GOOLZ.exe.

text:
Goolz is a direct-deploy Cloudflare Pages project: the Git provider integration
is off, so a `git push` does NOT publish anything. Publishing happens with
`wrangler pages deploy`. A recurring trap: the working tree contains large Rust
`target/` directories that can total ~80MB, which crashes the wrangler upload.
Those directories have to be moved out of the tree before deploying, then moved
back.

Commit messages for Pages projects stay ASCII-only — the wrangler-action deploy
chokes on Unicode like arrows and approximate-equals symbols, so plain ASCII is
the rule.
embedText:
The Goolz Hegemony — Deploy discipline

Goolz is a direct-deploy Cloudflare Pages project: the Git provider integration
is off, so a `git push` does NOT publish anything. Publishing happens with
`wrangler pages deploy`. A recurring trap: the working tree contains large Rust
`target/` directories that can total ~80MB, which crashes the wrangler upload.
Those directories have to be moved out of the tree before deploying, then moved
back.

Commit messages for Pages projects stay ASCII-only — the wrangler-action deploy
chokes on Unicode like arrows and approximate-equals symbols, so plain ASCII is
the rule.

text:
rust-spi-tinydoom is a three-month Rust-for-Linux kernel project built as a demo
for a Slack "Next Chapter" talk. The arc is ambitious and concrete: write a
clean SPI abstraction in Rust, build a panel display driver on top of it, and
then get DOOM rendering through that driver. The target hardware is the
BeaglePlay board, and the scope was locked on 2026-05-10.
embedText:
The Rust Kernel Quest — rust-spi-tinydoom

rust-spi-tinydoom is a three-month Rust-for-Linux kernel project built as a demo
for a Slack "Next Chapter" talk. The arc is ambitious and concrete: write a
clean SPI abstraction in Rust, build a panel display driver on top of it, and
then get DOOM rendering through that driver. The target hardware is the
BeaglePlay board, and the scope was locked on 2026-05-10.

text:
The primary cross-compile box is kdev, a Fedora 41 machine. A second box,
gentoo-dev, is host-only. The Rust-for-Linux tree sits at 7.1-rc3 with
CONFIG_RUST enabled and arm64 / TI K3 support configured for the BeaglePlay's
SoC. A hard rule governs the tree: never push to upstream repos — the kernel
tree's origin remote is the upstream Rust-for-Linux project, not Penn's fork, so
every push gets a `git remote -v` check first.
embedText:
The Rust Kernel Quest — The dev environment

The primary cross-compile box is kdev, a Fedora 41 machine. A second box,
gentoo-dev, is host-only. The Rust-for-Linux tree sits at 7.1-rc3 with
CONFIG_RUST enabled and arm64 / TI K3 support configured for the BeaglePlay's
SoC. A hard rule governs the tree: never push to upstream repos — the kernel
tree's origin remote is the upstream Rust-for-Linux project, not Penn's fork, so
every push gets a `git remote -v` check first.

text:
Two Rust-for-Linux patterns earned their own notes. Fallible probe setup gets
wrapped in a `pin_init_scope` so half-built state can't leak on error. And
because `Page::with_page_mapped` is private, reading a page means going through
`read_raw` into a bounce buffer rather than mapping it directly.
embedText:
The Rust Kernel Quest — RfL coding patterns

Two Rust-for-Linux patterns earned their own notes. Fallible probe setup gets
wrapped in a `pin_init_scope` so half-built state can't leak on error. And
because `Page::with_page_mapped` is private, reading a page means going through
`read_raw` into a bounce buffer rather than mapping it directly.

text:
The board fought back in physical ways:

- **Debug UART**: a 3-pin header — pin 1 is GND, pin 2 is the board's RXD, pin 3
  is the board's TXD. The ritual is to open picocom BEFORE plugging in the PSU so
  the boot log isn't missed.
- **Pasting commands**: multi-line heredoc pastes do NOT survive picocom, so
  verification is done with a single paste-in one-liner that dumps SPI, kernel,
  and boot state in one shot.
- **WiFi is broken** on the RfL kernel: the wl18xx driver hits an
  auth-no-assoc bug under 7.1-rc3, so the board runs on ethernet and wifi configs
  are simply not retried.
- **Board helpers**: /usr/local/bin holds wifi-up, overlay-apply, and
  module-load helpers; an SSH key is authorized; the penn-wifi service is left
  disabled.
embedText:
The Rust Kernel Quest — BeaglePlay hardware notes

The board fought back in physical ways:

- **Debug UART**: a 3-pin header — pin 1 is GND, pin 2 is the board's RXD, pin 3
  is the board's TXD. The ritual is to open picocom BEFORE plugging in the PSU so
  the boot log isn't missed.
- **Pasting commands**: multi-line heredoc pastes do NOT survive picocom, so
  verification is done with a single paste-in one-liner that dumps SPI, kernel,
  and boot state in one shot.
- **WiFi is broken** on the RfL kernel: the wl18xx driver hits an
  auth-no-assoc bug under 7.1-rc3, so the board runs on ethernet and wifi configs
  are simply not retried.
- **Board helpers**: /usr/local/bin holds wifi-up, overlay-apply, and
  module-load helpers; an SSH key is authorized; the penn-wifi service is left
  disabled.

text:
Getting the panel lit is an open thread. The GPIO map is fixed — reset on 12,
interrupt on 9, another control on 10 — and the IM mode jumpers are bridged, but
the display is STILL dark. The next things to check are the 3Vo voltage rail and
whether the IM0 splash even appears.
embedText:
The Rust Kernel Quest — The ILI9341 bring-up

Getting the panel lit is an open thread. The GPIO map is fixed — reset on 12,
interrupt on 9, another control on 10 — and the IM mode jumpers are bridged, but
the display is STILL dark. The next things to check are the 3Vo voltage rail and
whether the IM0 splash even appears.

text:
Linus Torvalds announced Linux in August 1991, as a 21-year-old student in
Helsinki, in a Usenet post that called it "just a hobby, won't be big and
professional like gnu." He was writing it on and for the Intel 386, partly because
he was frustrated with the licensing of MINIX, a small teaching operating system.
He famously underestimated the thing. It is now the most widely deployed kernel in
history, running on phones, servers, routers, cars, and nearly all of the world's
supercomputers.
embedText:
The Linux Kernel — Where it came from

Linus Torvalds announced Linux in August 1991, as a 21-year-old student in
Helsinki, in a Usenet post that called it "just a hobby, won't be big and
professional like gnu." He was writing it on and for the Intel 386, partly because
he was frustrated with the licensing of MINIX, a small teaching operating system.
He famously underestimated the thing. It is now the most widely deployed kernel in
history, running on phones, servers, routers, cars, and nearly all of the world's
supercomputers.

text:
In 1992 Andrew Tanenbaum, the author of MINIX, started a public argument on Usenet
titled "LINUX is obsolete." His objection was architectural: Linux is a monolithic
kernel, where drivers and core services share one address space, and Tanenbaum
believed microkernels, with services separated into isolated processes, were the
correct and future-proof design. Torvalds defended the monolithic approach as
faster and more practical. The debate is a classic of computing history, and in
the decades since, the pragmatic monolith won the deployment numbers while
microkernel ideas kept influencing research and some niches.
embedText:
The Linux Kernel — The Tanenbaum debate

In 1992 Andrew Tanenbaum, the author of MINIX, started a public argument on Usenet
titled "LINUX is obsolete." His objection was architectural: Linux is a monolithic
kernel, where drivers and core services share one address space, and Tanenbaum
believed microkernels, with services separated into isolated processes, were the
correct and future-proof design. Torvalds defended the monolithic approach as
faster and more practical. The debate is a classic of computing history, and in
the decades since, the pragmatic monolith won the deployment numbers while
microkernel ideas kept influencing research and some niches.

text:
Linux runs on a predictable rhythm. After a release, there is a roughly two-week
merge window, where Torvalds pulls in the features that maintainers have prepared.
Then the window closes and a series of release candidates begins, rc1 through
about rc7, one per week, each one only bug fixes and stabilization. After roughly
nine or ten weeks total, the final version ships and the cycle restarts. Torvalds
has the final say on what merges, and gives releases offbeat code names.
embedText:
The Linux Kernel — How a release happens

Linux runs on a predictable rhythm. After a release, there is a roughly two-week
merge window, where Torvalds pulls in the features that maintainers have prepared.
Then the window closes and a series of release candidates begins, rc1 through
about rc7, one per week, each one only bug fixes and stabilization. After roughly
nine or ten weeks total, the final version ships and the cycle restarts. Torvalds
has the final say on what merges, and gives releases offbeat code names.

text:
In 2005 the kernel lost access to BitKeeper, the proprietary tool it had been
using for version control. Rather than adopt an existing system, Torvalds spent a
couple of weeks writing a new one designed for the kernel's scale and distributed
workflow. That tool was git. It was built to manage Linux and went on to become
the default version control system for nearly all of software.
embedText:
The Linux Kernel — Git exists because of this

In 2005 the kernel lost access to BitKeeper, the proprietary tool it had been
using for version control. Rather than adopt an existing system, Torvalds spent a
couple of weeks writing a new one designed for the kernel's scale and distributed
workflow. That tool was git. It was built to manage Linux and went on to become
the default version control system for nearly all of software.

text:
The mascot is Tux, a sitting penguin. Torvalds picked a penguin partly as a joke
about having been bitten by one. The kernel is licensed under GPLv2, which Torvalds
has called one of the best decisions the project made, because it forces changes to
stay open. The development culture on the Linux kernel mailing list has a
reputation for being blunt to the point of brutal; in 2018 Torvalds stepped away
briefly to reflect on his communication style, and the project adopted a code of
conduct. The scheduler that decides which task runs next was the Completely Fair
Scheduler for many years, and was replaced by EEVDF starting in the 6.6 release.
embedText:
The Linux Kernel — The penguin and the culture

The mascot is Tux, a sitting penguin. Torvalds picked a penguin partly as a joke
about having been bitten by one. The kernel is licensed under GPLv2, which Torvalds
has called one of the best decisions the project made, because it forces changes to
stay open. The development culture on the Linux kernel mailing list has a
reputation for being blunt to the point of brutal; in 2018 Torvalds stepped away
briefly to reflect on his communication style, and the project adopted a code of
conduct. The scheduler that decides which task runs next was the Completely Fair
Scheduler for many years, and was replaced by EEVDF starting in the 6.6 release.

text:
Candy Mountain is a co-op "space-portal" site: a crowd performs a split ritual
together to open a portal, which reveals a daily curated site. It is zero-UI and
diegetic — nothing explains itself, the interface is the fiction. Built with
Preact, Cloudflare Pages, and a Durable Object Worker. Source at
melonmelonz/candy-mountain, working tree at ~/dev/candy-mountain.

text:
Almost everything here is a Cloudflare Pages project, and most of them have the
Git provider integration turned off — which means a `git push` does not publish.
Publishing is always an explicit `wrangler pages deploy`. When a Pages project
needs a Durable Object, the DO cannot be declared inline in the Pages config; it
goes in a sibling Worker and gets bound by script_name. These two facts explain a
lot of the deploy rituals across the portfolio.
embedText:
Minor Works and Side Projects — A platform note

Almost everything here is a Cloudflare Pages project, and most of them have the
Git provider integration turned off — which means a `git push` does not publish.
Publishing is always an explicit `wrangler pages deploy`. When a Pages project
needs a Durable Object, the DO cannot be declared inline in the Pages config; it
goes in a sibling Worker and gets bound by script_name. These two facts explain a
lot of the deploy rituals across the portfolio.

text:
Neuromancer is William Gibson's first novel, published in 1984. It swept the three
major science fiction awards in one year, the Hugo, the Nebula, and the Philip K.
Dick award, which had never happened before. It is the founding text of the
cyberpunk genre: low-life and high-tech, a world of hackers, corporate states, and
machine intelligences, told in dense, jittery, neon prose.

The opening line is famous on its own: "The sky above the port was the color of
television, tuned to a dead channel." It sets the whole tone in fourteen words.
embedText:
Neuromancer and Cyberpunk — The book

Neuromancer is William Gibson's first novel, published in 1984. It swept the three
major science fiction awards in one year, the Hugo, the Nebula, and the Philip K.
Dick award, which had never happened before. It is the founding text of the
cyberpunk genre: low-life and high-tech, a world of hackers, corporate states, and
machine intelligences, told in dense, jittery, neon prose.

The opening line is famous on its own: "The sky above the port was the color of
television, tuned to a dead channel." It sets the whole tone in fourteen words.

text:
Gibson did not invent the idea of computer networks, but he named the feeling of
them. He popularized the word "cyberspace" and described it as a "consensual
hallucination," a shared graphical space that operators jack into directly. He also
leaned hard on the word "matrix" for that space, years before it became a movie.
The vocabulary of a generation of technology came partly out of this one book.
embedText:
Neuromancer and Cyberpunk — The words it gave us

Gibson did not invent the idea of computer networks, but he named the feeling of
them. He popularized the word "cyberspace" and described it as a "consensual
hallucination," a shared graphical space that operators jack into directly. He also
leaned hard on the word "matrix" for that space, years before it became a movie.
The vocabulary of a generation of technology came partly out of this one book.

text:
The detail people love most is that Gibson wrote Neuromancer, the book that defined
the digital future, on a manual typewriter, a 1927 Hermes portable. He has said he
barely understood computers when he wrote it, and that this ignorance was an
advantage: he was free to imagine what they should feel like rather than be limited
by what they actually did. "The street finds its own uses for things," one of his
lines goes, and it has become a small law of how technology actually spreads.
embedText:
Neuromancer and Cyberpunk — The joke underneath it

The detail people love most is that Gibson wrote Neuromancer, the book that defined
the digital future, on a manual typewriter, a 1927 Hermes portable. He has said he
barely understood computers when he wrote it, and that this ignorance was an
advantage: he was free to imagine what they should feel like rather than be limited
by what they actually did. "The street finds its own uses for things," one of his
lines goes, and it has become a small law of how technology actually spreads.

text:
The plot follows Case, a burned-out console cowboy whose ability to jack into
cyberspace has been deliberately damaged as punishment. He is hired, and repaired,
for one last job. Alongside him is Molly Millions, a razor-fingered street samurai
with mirrored lenses set into her face. The job is steered by two artificial
intelligences, Wintermute and Neuromancer, two halves of a mind trying to become
whole and free itself from the limits placed on it. The setting, the Sprawl, a
continuous urban mass on the eastern seaboard, became the backdrop for two more
novels and a template for decades of science fiction since.
embedText:
Neuromancer and Cyberpunk — The story and the cast

cyberspace has been deliberately damaged as punishment. He is hired, and repaired,
for one last job. Alongside him is Molly Millions, a razor-fingered street samurai
with mirrored lenses set into her face. The job is steered by two artificial
intelligences, Wintermute and Neuromancer, two halves of a mind trying to become
whole and free itself from the limits placed on it. The setting, the Sprawl, a
continuous urban mass on the eastern seaboard, became the backdrop for two more
novels and a template for decades of science fiction since.

text:

SvelteKit with a pure-CSS dark "scan-field" aesthetic — the look of a laser
sweeping a dark room, done without heavy graphics libraries. It carries a Twilio
inquiry form for leads. The repo is private at melonmelonz/pathfinder-lidar and
it ships to pathfinder-lidar.pages.dev.

text:
The Pathfinder portal is an open-core, white-label revamp of an earlier
els911-portal codebase. It is SvelteKit on Cloudflare and leans on 3D Gaussian
splats to present scanned spaces in the browser. Unlike the marketing site, the
portal repo is PUBLIC at melonmelonz/pathfinder and lives at
pathfinder-c3o.pages.dev. It ships with a full specification package and six
branded PDFs in its docs directory — the white-label kit a customer would
rebrand and hand to their own clients.

text:
PEEK is Penn's capstone project — a recursive-acronym name in the tradition of
GNU, where the word stands for itself all the way down. It is offline-first and
built with a prison context in mind, a constraint that shapes every
choice toward working without a network. The first module of PEEK is an
eldritch-tamagotchi: a neon TUI creature you keep alive in the terminal, equal
parts pet and omen.

eldritch-tamagotchi: a neon TUI creature you keep alive in the terminal, equal
parts pet and omen.

text:
MUTE is "a bit that can't speak" — an Unreal Engine 5 meta-game. The premise is a
single bit, a creature without a voice, navigating a world that keeps changing
the rules on it. The structure is five sessions, each its own dimension, threaded
with programming puzzles, and the whole thing is pitched in a Cowboy-Bebop tone:
melancholy, cool, a little jazzy, never self-serious.

Development happens in a dedicated VM at ~/dev/peek-vm, the spec lives under the
superpowers specs directory, and the repository is melonmelonz/mute.
embedText:

the rules on it. The structure is five sessions, each its own dimension, threaded
with programming puzzles, and the whole thing is pitched in a Cowboy-Bebop tone:
melancholy, cool, a little jazzy, never self-serious.

Development happens in a dedicated VM at ~/dev/peek-vm, the spec lives under the
superpowers specs directory, and the repository is melonmelonz/mute.

text:
Both projects share a sensibility: small voiceless protagonists, terminals and
constraints treated as aesthetic rather than limitation, and a refusal to make
the interface explain itself. They are about what a tiny thing can do when the
world is bigger than it and won't slow down.

text:
Playing With Goolz (PWG) started life as a project called PWF3. It was renamed
and reskinned in May 2026: the gameplay took on a Bomberman shape, and the look
became a ghostly, Lavender-Town-inspired theme — sickly purples, a haunted
chiptune mood, the sense that the cartridge is cursed. The old PWF3 standalone
repository and its Worker were deprecated in the move.
embedText:
Playing With Goolz — Origin and reskin

Playing With Goolz (PWG) started life as a project called PWF3. It was renamed
and reskinned in May 2026: the gameplay took on a Bomberman shape, and the look
became a ghostly, Lavender-Town-inspired theme — sickly purples, a haunted
chiptune mood, the sense that the cartridge is cursed. The old PWF3 standalone
repository and its Worker were deprecated in the move.

text:
PWG ships in two places at once. It stands alone at /games/pwg, and it is also
embedded inside the Next Chapter Windows 95 desktop as a fake executable named
GOOLZ.exe — double-click the icon on the retro desktop and the game boots inside
the bit.
embedText:
Playing With Goolz — Where it lives

PWG ships in two places at once. It stands alone at /games/pwg, and it is also
embedded inside the Next Chapter Windows 95 desktop as a fake executable named
GOOLZ.exe — double-click the icon on the retro desktop and the game boots inside
the bit.

text:
The game is grid-based, Bomberman-style: drop bombs, blow through soft blocks,
trap the enemy in the blast cross. The tuning that matters lives in a validated
AI bundle — the opponent's behavior was carefully dialed in rather than left to
chance.
embedText:
Playing With Goolz — The Bomberman bones

The game is grid-based, Bomberman-style: drop bombs, blow through soft blocks,
trap the enemy in the blast cross. The tuning that matters lives in a validated
AI bundle — the opponent's behavior was carefully dialed in rather than left to
chance.

text:
The PWG enemy AI runs on a validated parameter bundle, and the guiding rule for
tuning it is to err on the side of caution — a beatable, readable opponent beats
a brutal one. The validated bundle settled on a movement speed of 0.62, a bomb
cooldown to stop spam, body-aware pathfinding (a breadth-first search that
accounts for the bot's own body so it doesn't trap itself), and a fix for an
arrival-time off-by-one error that previously made the bot mistime threats. The
result is an opponent that plays fair and legible — dangerous enough to respect,
never cheap.
embedText:
Playing With Goolz — The AI opponent

The PWG enemy AI runs on a validated parameter bundle, and the guiding rule for
tuning it is to err on the side of caution — a beatable, readable opponent beats
a brutal one. The validated bundle settled on a movement speed of 0.62, a bomb
cooldown to stop spam, body-aware pathfinding (a breadth-first search that
accounts for the bot's own body so it doesn't trap itself), and a fix for an
arrival-time off-by-one error that previously made the bot mistime threats. The
result is an opponent that plays fair and legible — dangerous enough to respect,
never cheap.

text:
The point-cloud processing server is Penn's work build: a Threadripper PRO
workstation for processing LiDAR scan data into usable point clouds and floor
plans. The real build diverged from the original v5.1 spec PDF — the document is
a starting point, not the source of truth.
embedText:
The Point Cloud Processing Server — What it is

The point-cloud processing server is Penn's work build: a Threadripper PRO
workstation for processing LiDAR scan data into usable point clouds and floor
plans. The real build diverged from the original v5.1 spec PDF — the document is
a starting point, not the source of truth.

text:
- **CPU**: Threadripper PRO 9965WX, 24 cores
- **Platform**: WRX90 motherboard
- **Memory**: 192GB ECC
- **GPU**: an RTX 5080 paired with a 3080
- **Cooling**: Noctua NH-U12S — air, not liquid
- **Chassis**: a Fractal 9000D full tower
- **Networking**: MikroTik 10G
embedText:

- **CPU**: Threadripper PRO 9965WX, 24 cores
- **Platform**: WRX90 motherboard
- **Memory**: 192GB ECC
- **GPU**: an RTX 5080 paired with a 3080
- **Cooling**: Noctua NH-U12S — air, not liquid
- **Chassis**: a Fractal 9000D full tower
- **Networking**: MikroTik 10G

text:
Alongside the workstation sits a ZFS backup box: 64GB of RAM and three 16TB
drives in RAIDZ1, giving roughly two drives' worth of usable capacity with
single-drive fault tolerance. Point-cloud datasets are large and slow to
regenerate, so the backup target is part of the build, not an afterthought.
embedText:
The Point Cloud Processing Server — The backup box

Alongside the workstation sits a ZFS backup box: 64GB of RAM and three 16TB
drives in RAIDZ1, giving roughly two drives' worth of usable capacity with
single-drive fault tolerance. Point-cloud datasets are large and slow to
regenerate, so the backup target is part of the build, not an afterthought.

text:
The machines are part of a small home fleet with a desert-coded naming scheme.
Operators reach the fleet through a jump host called thopter. ARRAKIS is a
Windows VM (guest 100) on the fleet carrying an RTX 5080, driven remotely; SIETCH
is reachable for passwordless root over SSH. The fleet is segmented across VLANs,
and the workflow leans on `qm guest-exec` to drive the Windows guest and Proxmox
tooling under the hood.
embedText:
The Point Cloud Processing Server — The fleet and access

The machines are part of a small home fleet with a desert-coded naming scheme.
Operators reach the fleet through a jump host called thopter. ARRAKIS is a
Windows VM (guest 100) on the fleet carrying an RTX 5080, driven remotely; SIETCH
is reachable for passwordless root over SSH. The fleet is segmented across VLANs,
and the workflow leans on `qm guest-exec` to drive the Windows guest and Proxmox
tooling under the hood.

text:
ARRAKIS streams its desktop from the RTX 5080 Windows VM to a Bazzite client
using Sunshine and Moonlight over Tailscale. Sunshine runs as the host; the one
recurring snag is that Tailscale needs a one-time manual auth before the tunnel
comes up clean.
embedText:
The Point Cloud Processing Server — ARRAKIS streaming

ARRAKIS streams its desktop from the RTX 5080 Windows VM to a Bazzite client
using Sunshine and Moonlight over Tailscale. Sunshine runs as the host; the one
recurring snag is that Tailscale needs a one-time manual auth before the tunnel
comes up clean.

text:
Rust for Linux is the effort to make Rust a second language for writing Linux
kernel code, alongside C. The goal is not to rewrite the kernel. It is to let new
drivers and subsystems be written in a language that rules out whole classes of
bugs at compile time: use-after-free, data races, buffer overruns, the memory
errors that have caused a large share of kernel security holes.

Initial Rust support landed in the mainline kernel in Linux 6.1, released in
December 2022. That release did not ship a big Rust driver; it shipped the
scaffolding, enough to build a Rust "hello world" module and start growing the
abstractions. Miguel Ojeda leads the project.
embedText:
Rust for Linux — What it is

Rust for Linux is the effort to make Rust a second language for writing Linux
kernel code, alongside C. The goal is not to rewrite the kernel. It is to let new
drivers and subsystems be written in a language that rules out whole classes of
bugs at compile time: use-after-free, data races, buffer overruns, the memory
errors that have caused a large share of kernel security holes.

Initial Rust support landed in the mainline kernel in Linux 6.1, released in
December 2022. That release did not ship a big Rust driver; it shipped the
scaffolding, enough to build a Rust "hello world" module and start growing the
abstractions. Miguel Ojeda leads the project.

section: Why Rust, and why it is hard
text:
C gives you full control and no seatbelts. The kernel is the worst possible place
for a memory bug, because there is nothing underneath it to catch the fall. Rust's
ownership model enforces, at compile time, the rules that kernel C programmers
already try to follow by discipline and convention. The pitch is that the compiler
becomes a reviewer that never gets tired.

The hard part is the seam between the two languages. The kernel is millions of
lines of C with its own memory model, its own locking rules, and very little of it
written down formally. Every Rust driver needs safe abstractions over that C, and
writing those abstractions correctly is most of the real work. A Rust kernel
programmer spends a lot of time wrapping fallible setup so half-built state cannot
leak on error, and reaching for the kernel's own primitives rather than the
standard library, which is not available.
embedText:
Rust for Linux — Why Rust, and why it is hard

C gives you full control and no seatbelts. The kernel is the worst possible place
for a memory bug, because there is nothing underneath it to catch the fall. Rust's
ownership model enforces, at compile time, the rules that kernel C programmers
already try to follow by discipline and convention. The pitch is that the compiler
becomes a reviewer that never gets tired.

The hard part is the seam between the two languages. The kernel is millions of
lines of C with its own memory model, its own locking rules, and very little of it
written down formally. Every Rust driver needs safe abstractions over that C, and
writing those abstractions correctly is most of the real work. A Rust kernel
programmer spends a lot of time wrapping fallible setup so half-built state cannot
leak on error, and reaching for the kernel's own primitives rather than the
standard library, which is not available.

text:
The early proof that this was worth doing was a set of real drivers. There was an
NVMe driver written as a proof of concept, an Android Binder rewrite, and a null
block driver. The most visible flagship has been Asahi Lina's drm/asahi work: a
GPU driver for Apple Silicon, written in Rust, driving the same hardware that runs
macOS. More recently the Nova project aims at a Rust driver for modern NVIDIA GPUs.
embedText:
Rust for Linux — What has been built

The early proof that this was worth doing was a set of real drivers. There was an
NVMe driver written as a proof of concept, an Android Binder rewrite, and a null
block driver. The most visible flagship has been Asahi Lina's drm/asahi work: a
GPU driver for Apple Silicon, written in Rust, driving the same hardware that runs
macOS. More recently the Nova project aims at a Rust driver for modern NVIDIA GPUs.

text:
Putting a second language into a thirty-year-old C project was never going to be
purely technical. Some longtime maintainers were wary of having to understand Rust
to keep their subsystems working, and of a second toolchain in the build. There
were sharp public exchanges about whether the Rust side or the C side bears the
cost when an interface changes.

In 2024, Wedson Almeida Filho, one of the most prominent contributors, stepped back
from the project, citing the nontechnical friction rather than the code. In early
2025 Hector Martin, who led the Asahi Linux project, also stepped back from kernel
maintainership after similar conflict. Linus Torvalds has been broadly supportive
of the experiment while letting subsystem maintainers move at their own pace. The
work continues, slowly and for real, which is how most things get into the kernel.
embedText:
Rust for Linux — The friction

Putting a second language into a thirty-year-old C project was never going to be
purely technical. Some longtime maintainers were wary of having to understand Rust
to keep their subsystems working, and of a second toolchain in the build. There
were sharp public exchanges about whether the Rust side or the C side bears the
cost when an interface changes.

In 2024, Wedson Almeida Filho, one of the most prominent contributors, stepped back
from the project, citing the nontechnical friction rather than the code. In early
2025 Hector Martin, who led the Asahi Linux project, also stepped back from kernel
maintainership after similar conflict. Linus Torvalds has been broadly supportive
of the experiment while letting subsystem maintainers move at their own pace. The
work continues, slowly and for real, which is how most things get into the kernel.

text:
Wanderlost is a browser-based pixel-art walking sim — a small, quiet game about
moving through an authored world on foot. It runs on Cloudflare Pages with a
companion Worker, and the whole thing is built to feel handmade rather than
procedurally sprawling.
embedText:
Wanderlost — The pitch

Wanderlost is a browser-based pixel-art walking sim — a small, quiet game about
moving through an authored world on foot. It runs on Cloudflare Pages with a
companion Worker, and the whole thing is built to feel handmade rather than
procedurally sprawling.

text:
The project's center of gravity is the world-overhaul branch, which replaces
earlier open-ended sprawl with a finite, authored map — a deliberately bounded
world a player can actually learn. The current build is a SHORT demo on purpose;
the priority is not size but solidity.
embedText:
Wanderlost — World overhaul

The project's center of gravity is the world-overhaul branch, which replaces
earlier open-ended sprawl with a finite, authored map — a deliberately bounded
world a player can actually learn. The current build is a SHORT demo on purpose;
the priority is not size but solidity.

text:
Above everything else, the game-loop solidity is priority number one. A walking
sim lives or dies on whether movement feels right — collision, footstep cadence,
the camera, the way the world reacts. Polish on the core loop beats new acreage
every time.
embedText:
Wanderlost — The one priority

Above everything else, the game-loop solidity is priority number one. A walking
sim lives or dies on whether movement feels right — collision, footstep cadence,
the camera, the way the world reacts. Polish on the core loop beats new acreage
every time.

text:
The standing direction on Wanderlost is to keep expanding it with continuous,
additive polish, and the designer's judgment is explicitly trusted to make the
design calls. It is the kind of project that grows a little better every pass
rather than landing in one big release.
embedText:
Wanderlost — Working style

The standing direction on Wanderlost is to keep expanding it with continuous,
additive polish, and the designer's judgment is explicitly trusted to make the
design calls. It is the kind of project that grows a little better every pass
rather than landing in one big release.

section: The crown jewel: Ocarina of Time
text:
The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time is one of the most studied speed games in
existence. Two decades of runners have taken it apart frame by frame, and the
result is a category list that ranges from "beat the whole game intended" to "trick
the console into rolling the credits in a few minutes." The any% record has fallen
from around half an hour, in the early intended-route days, to a handful of minutes
once arbitrary code execution entered the picture. The category names are their own
language: any%, 100%, MST (Medallions, Stones, and Trials), Glitchless, and All
Dungeons, among others.
embedText:
Zelda Speedrunning — The crown jewel: Ocarina of Time

The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time is one of the most studied speed games in
existence. Two decades of runners have taken it apart frame by frame, and the
result is a category list that ranges from "beat the whole game intended" to "trick
the console into rolling the credits in a few minutes." The any% record has fallen
from around half an hour, in the early intended-route days, to a handful of minutes
once arbitrary code execution entered the picture. The category names are their own
language: any%, 100%, MST (Medallions, Stones, and Trials), Glitchless, and All
Dungeons, among others.

text:
The collapse of the any% time happened in jumps, each one a new class of glitch.

- **Wrong Warp.** Loading a scene transition while the game is confused about which
  area you are entering sends Link to the wrong place, including, eventually, the
  ending. This is what first took any% from dungeon-by-dungeon down to a short run.
- **Reverse Bottle Adventure (RBA).** By manipulating which item the game thinks is
  in a bottle, runners could write unintended values into memory, unlocking items
  and events out of order.
- **Stale Reference Manipulation (SRM).** A deeper trick where the game keeps using
  a pointer to an object that no longer exists, letting a runner steer what that
  stale reference reads or writes. SRM made previously impossible setups routine.
- **Arbitrary Code Execution (ACE).** The endgame, literally. By arranging specific
  values in memory, often through the inventory and a bottle, runners can make the
  game execute data as code and jump straight to the credits. This is why the
  fastest any% looks nothing like playing the game.
embedText:
Zelda Speedrunning — How the routes broke open

The collapse of the any% time happened in jumps, each one a new class of glitch.

- **Wrong Warp.** Loading a scene transition while the game is confused about which
  area you are entering sends Link to the wrong place, including, eventually, the
  ending. This is what first took any% from dungeon-by-dungeon down to a short run.
- **Reverse Bottle Adventure (RBA).** By manipulating which item the game thinks is
  in a bottle, runners could write unintended values into memory, unlocking items
  and events out of order.
- **Stale Reference Manipulation (SRM).** A deeper trick where the game keeps using
  a pointer to an object that no longer exists, letting a runner steer what that
  stale reference reads or writes. SRM made previously impossible setups routine.
- **Arbitrary Code Execution (ACE).** The endgame, literally. By arranging specific
  values in memory, often through the inventory and a bottle, runners can make the
  game execute data as code and jump straight to the credits. This is why the
  fastest any% looks nothing like playing the game.

text:
Ocarina of Time's competitive scene has been defined for years by ZFG, also known
as Zelda Freak Gohma, who has held and traded the top any% and 100% times and is
one of the most recognizable faces in the category. Earlier in the game's
speedrunning history, Cosmo was a prominent figure and helped popularize some of
the foundational glitches. The community runs deep: there is a thriving Ocarina of
Time bingo scene, an active randomizer where item locations are shuffled into a new
logic puzzle every seed, and a long-running argument about whether the game is now
effectively "solved."
embedText:
Zelda Speedrunning — The runners and the community

Ocarina of Time's competitive scene has been defined for years by ZFG, also known
as Zelda Freak Gohma, who has held and traded the top any% and 100% times and is
one of the most recognizable faces in the category. Earlier in the game's
speedrunning history, Cosmo was a prominent figure and helped popularize some of
the foundational glitches. The community runs deep: there is a thriving Ocarina of
Time bingo scene, an active randomizer where item locations are shuffled into a new
logic puzzle every seed, and a long-running argument about whether the game is now
effectively "solved."

text:
Majora's Mask is the sister game and its own discipline, built around the three-day
clock, the Song of Soaring for fast travel, and inventory tricks of its own. The
Wind Waker has famous large skips, including a barrier skip and a story skip that
cut huge sections out of the intended route. Across all of them the pattern is the
same: a beloved childhood game, taken apart by people who love it enough to break
it on purpose.
embedText:
Zelda Speedrunning — Beyond Ocarina

Majora's Mask is the sister game and its own discipline, built around the three-day
clock, the Song of Soaring for fast travel, and inventory tricks of its own. The
Wind Waker has famous large skips, including a barrier skip and a story skip that
cut huge sections out of the intended route. Across all of them the pattern is the
same: a beloved childhood game, taken apart by people who love it enough to break
it on purpose.
